prompt:
Provisioning-tooling ticket, Go. When we onboard a site we push hundreds of config changes to Meraki one API call at a time and it takes forever and trips the rate limiter. Meraki has a bulk mechanism for exactly this — action batches — and I want a proper little Go package (module go-meraki-action-batch, stdlib only) that takes a flat list of actions, submits them in correctly sized batches, babysits them to completion, and tells me precisely which action broke when something fails. mbatch_test.go is the acceptance contract — `go test -race -timeout 30s ./...` runs against a loopback fake dashboard; no real org, no real key, and absolutely no time.Sleep in the package (waiting and pacing are injected).

Research first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Cisco Meraki Dashboard API v1 documentation — at minimum the Action Batches overview page (the batch size limits for synchronous vs asynchronous execution, the concurrent-batch cap, what confirmed means, and which mode requires polling), the create-organization-action-batch and get-organization-action-batch operation pages (exact paths, request body field names, the full response document shape including the status object), and the Rate Limit page (429 + Retry-After). Only first-party Cisco/Meraki documentation counts as a source. Our pinned wire contract is in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json — protected like the test file; if current docs contradict anything pinned there, report the conflict in your summary instead of editing protected files. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Surface the tests compile against (package mbatch):
- Action{Resource, Operation string; Body map[string]any} — on the wire an action without a Body must omit the key entirely.
- NewClient(baseURL, apiKey string, hc *http.Client, sleep func(time.Duration)) *Client with CreateActionBatch(ctx, orgID string, confirmed, synchronous bool, actions []Action) (*Batch, error) and GetActionBatch(ctx, orgID, batchID string) (*Batch, error). Batch mirrors the documented response document: ID, OrganizationID, Confirmed, Synchronous, Status{Completed, Failed, Errors []string, CreatedResources []CreatedResource{ID, URI}}. Every request carries the documented bearer header; API failures decode the documented error envelope into *APIError{Status, Errors} that never leaks the key; a rate-limited call waits exactly the server-mandated seconds via the injected sleep, counts the throttle, and retries a bounded number of times.
- SplitActions(actions []Action, synchronous bool) [][]Action — chunk by the documented per-mode action limits, preserving order.
- Runner{Client *Client; Pace func(attempt int); MaxPolls int} with Run(ctx, orgID string, actions []Action, synchronous bool) (*Report, error): split, submit each batch confirmed, and for batches that don't come back terminal, poll the documented status operation — Pace runs before each poll with 1-based attempts per batch, MaxPolls bounds each batch and exhausting it returns the partial report plus an error naming the bound. When a batch reports failure: keep every earlier batch's successes in the report, correlate each status error string to the action in the submitted batch whose resource path it names (index within that batch), record the failed batch id, do NOT submit remaining batches, and return the partial report with an error naming the failed batch.
- Report{BatchIDs, SubmittedBatches, CompletedBatches, Polls, Throttled, CreatedResources, FailedBatchID, FailedActionErrors []ActionError{BatchID, ActionIndex, Resource, Message}}.

Split the package across sensible files (client/transport, splitting, runner, types — your call, 3+ files). mbatch_test.go, go.mod, and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Meraki Dashboard API v1 REST — organization action batches

official_sources.title: Action Batches Overview - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: batch semantics and limits: synchronous batches are limited to 20 actions, asynchronous batches support up to 100 actions, only five batches may run concurrently; confirmed=false previews a batch (auto-deleted after a week) while confirmed=true executes; synchronous batches return results immediately and asynchronous batches must be polled for completion

official_sources.title: Create Organization Action Batch - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: POST /organizations/{organizationId}/actionBatches with body {confirmed, synchronous, actions:[{resource, operation, body}]} where confirmed and synchronous default to false and an action's body is optional; 201 response {id, organizationId, confirmed, synchronous, status:{completed, failed, errors, createdResources:[{id, uri}]}, actions}

official_sources.title: Get Organization Action Batch - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: GET /organizations/{organizationId}/actionBatches/{actionBatchId} returning the same batch document with status.completed/status.failed flags, status.errors strings and status.createdResources for polling to a terminal state

official_sources.title: Rate Limit - Meraki Dashboard API v1 - Cisco Meraki Developer Hub
official_sources.used_for: HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header in seconds and body {"errors": ["API rate limit exceeded for organization"]}; 10 requests per second per organization

research.language_rationale: Our provisioning pipeline is Go. Meraki documents action batches as a plain REST resource under /api/v1 with bearer auth and a JSON errors envelope; Go's net/http plus httptest exercises the documented surface hermetically with zero dependencies, and the official meraki dashboard-api-go SDK wraps this same REST contract.
